summary: Distance/rate/time problems and combined work problems both use the same idea: rates add when working together, and rate x time = amount done.
explanation: For motion problems, distance equals rate times time (\(d=rt\)); rearrange to find whichever quantity is unknown. For work problems, express each worker's rate as "1 job per T time" (e.g. a machine that takes 4 hours has a rate of \(1/4\) job per hour). When two agents work together, their rates add: combined rate \(= 1/t_1 + 1/t_2\), and the time to finish together is the reciprocal of that sum. A common shortcut for two workers with times \(a\) and \(b\): time together \(= \dfrac{ab}{a+b}\). For average speed over a round trip with different speeds each way, don't average the speeds directly — use total distance divided by total time.
